text:
CT NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old woman with history of HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive staging), treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation (IMRT 70 Gy in 35 fractions, completed approximately 6 months prior). Presenting for routine post-treatment surveillance imaging. Prior PET-CT performed at 12 weeks post-treatment demonstrated complete metabolic response. Evaluate for recurrence.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the neck acquired from the skull base through the thoracic inlet before and after the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 iohexol intravenous contrast. Images reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes at 2.5 mm slice thickness. Multiplanar reformats reviewed. Comparison made to prior PET-CT (approximately 3 months earlier) and prior CT neck (performed at initial staging).

---

FINDINGS:

PRIMARY SITE — BASE OF TONGUE / OROPHARYNX:
The left base of tongue demonstrates expected post-radiation mucosal thickening and mild submucosal edema without discrete mass lesion or focal enhancement pattern suspicious for residual or recurrent tumor. The mucosa is smooth in contour. There is no identifiable discrete enhancing nodularity or ulceration at the left base of tongue. Comparison with the staging MRI, which demonstrated a 2.2 x 1.9 cm enhancing left base of tongue mass, confirms marked interval change. The right base of tongue and bilateral tonsillar pillars are unremarkable. The vallecula and epiglottis appear within normal limits. Pre-epiglottic and paraglottic spaces are preserved without infiltrative change.

CERVICAL LYMPH NODES:
The previously enlarged left level IIA lymph node has continued to decrease and now measures approximately 0.7 cm in short-axis dimension, with restoration of a normal, ovoid morphology and no internal low-density change to suggest necrosis. This is a substantial reduction from the initial presentation (short-axis 1.8 cm with internal necrosis at staging). No abnormally enlarged or morphologically suspicious cervical lymph nodes are identified bilaterally across levels IA, IB, II, III, IV, V, or VI. No retropharyngeal nodal enlargement. The right neck is unremarkable.

SALIVARY GLANDS:
Bilateral parotid glands demonstrate diffuse mild volume reduction consistent with post-radiation atrophic change. Parotid parenchyma is somewhat heterogeneous in attenuation bilaterally, a finding expected following prior radiation therapy with attempted parotid sparing. No discrete parotid mass or suspicious enhancement. Submandibular glands are similarly mildly diminished in volume bilaterally, with no focal lesion identified. No obstructive sialadenitis or ductal dilatation.

PHARYNX AND LARYNX:
Oropharynx: diffuse circumferential mild mucosal thickening present, consistent with post-radiation inflammatory change. No focal mass or asymmetric thickening suspicious for tumor. Hypopharynx is symmetric and unremarkable. Larynx: true and false vocal cords appear symmetric without mass, ulceration, or subglottic extension. Arytenoids are well seen without fixation or abnormal soft tissue fullness.

SOFT TISSUES OF THE NECK:
Diffuse soft tissue thickening and increased stranding of the bilateral anterior and lateral cervical subcutaneous fat, particularly prominent in the mid and lower neck, consistent with post-radiation fibrosis and chronic inflammatory change. Bilateral sternocleidomastoid muscles show mild heterogeneity without frank necrosis or pathologic enhancement. No drainable fluid collection or abscess. Skin thickening of the bilateral anterior neck is noted, consistent with prior radiation field changes. No subcutaneous nodularity suspicious for recurrence.

THYROID GLAND:
Thyroid gland is mildly heterogeneous in echotexture with overall slightly reduced volume, consistent with post-radiation thyroiditis/evolving change. No discrete nodule greater than 1 cm in the dominant dimension. No suspicious calcification pattern. Clinical correlation with thyroid function tests recommended given history of post-radiation hypothyroidism.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
Bilateral carotid arteries are widely patent without flow-limiting stenosis. No perivascular soft tissue infiltration. Internal jugular veins are patent bilaterally.

BONY STRUCTURES:
The cervical vertebral bodies are intact. No lytic or sclerotic lesion identified at the imaged osseous structures. Mandible is intact without erosion or periosteal reaction.

INCIDENTAL FINDINGS:
Limited evaluation of the lung apices reveals no pulmonary nodule or suspicious density within the imaged field of view. A small cervical lymph node at right level III measuring 0.5 cm is within normal limits for size and morphology.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No discrete mass, abnormal enhancement, or morphologic finding to suggest recurrent or residual oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma at the primary left base of tongue site. Post-treatment mucosal thickening and soft tissue edema are present and expected at this interval.

2. The previously pathologically enlarged left level IIA cervical lymph node has continued to decrease in size (now 0.7 cm, ovoid, without internal necrosis), consistent with ongoing favorable treatment response. No new or suspicious cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

3. Bilateral cervical soft tissue fibrosis, parotid gland atrophy, and submandibular gland volume reduction consistent with sequelae of prior radiation therapy.

4. Mildly heterogeneous and slightly small thyroid gland, consistent with post-radiation change in the setting of known evolving hypothyroidism.

5. No sonographically suspicious bony lesion or vascular complication identified.

Overall, imaging findings at 6 months post-treatment are reassuring and show no radiographic evidence of locoregional recurrence. Continued surveillance per clinical protocol.

Dictated and reviewed by: Attending Radiologist, Head and Neck Radiology
Electronically signed.
